images.caption: Figure 2:GRPO’s group-feasibility failure onHTH_{T}(latter 20% of MC,G=16G=16) averaged across 3 seeds.(a) Candidate background rates;green/reddenote feasible/infeasible w.r.t. the tolerance band. (b) Per-step feasible fractionft=nfeas/Gf_{t}=n_{\text{feas}}/G(run mean⟨ft⟩=0.58\langle f_{t}\rangle=0.58).ft=0f_{t}{=}0on30.8%30.8\%of steps, so the group has no in-band sample and GRPO reinforces the least-infeasible (still out-of-band) action. AD trigger is in Figure17.

Figure 3:Background rates forHTH_{T}(evaluated on 20% MC). Our methods (GFPO-F and GFPO-FR) concentrate the background rate inside the[90,110][90,110]kHztolerance band around the100100kHz target, while the baselines spread welloutsideit. GFPO-F sits on target(μ=100.0\mu=100.0kHz) with the tighter distribution (σ=2.0\sigma=2.0kHz). GFPO-FRruns at a slightly higher mean (μ=106.1\mu=106.1kHz) with a marginally wider spread(σ=3.4\sigma=3.4kHz).

Each step is classified by|Ft||F_{t}|relative toKK:Pure feasible (|Ft|≥K|F_{t}|\geq K, kept set𝒦t⊆ℱt\mathcal{K}_{t}\subseteq\mathcal{F}_{t}), Padded (0<|Ft|<K0<|F_{t}|<K, kept set mixes feasible and out-of-band candidates), Zero feasibility (ℱt=∅\mathcal{F}_{t}=\emptyset).AD trigger is in Figure18.

(a)GFPO dominates rate control on UNSW-NB15.Period 1-trained policies deployed frozen on Period 2 (solid) or with online training (hatched). Fraction of chunks in theFAR(False Alert Rate) target band.

(a)GFPO dominates rate control on UNSW-NB15.Period 1-trained policies deployed frozen on Period 2 (solid) or with online training (hatched). Fraction of chunks in theFAR(False Alert Rate) target band.

images.caption: Figure 8:Signal-background discrimination for theHTH_{T}and AD triggers (Full MC, no train/test split). (a)HTH_{T}score and(b) AD score distributions for background (MinBias, shaded blue),h→4​bh\to 4bsignal (redsolid), andt​t¯t\bar{t}signal (green dashed). The dashed black line indicates the oracle cut corresponding to theupper edge of the tolerance band (r+=0.275%r^{+}=0.275\%). (c) ROC curves showing signal efficiency versusbackground raterrfor both triggers and both signals. The light blue shaded region denotes the tolerance band(r∗±τ=0.25%±0.025%r^{*}\pm\tau=0.25\%\pm 0.025\%). Markers at the upper band edge show the maximum achievable signal efficiency:HTH_{T}trigger yields 98.3% fort​t¯t\bar{t}and 27.5% forh→4​bh\rightarrow 4b, while the AD trigger yields 94.4% fort​t¯t\bar{t}and 23.7% forh→4​bh\rightarrow 4b. The large gap between the two signals reflects the inherent overlapof theh→4​bh\rightarrow 4bdistribution with the MinimumBias background in both feature spaces, setting a physics-limitedupper bound on achievable signal efficiency at this operating point.

Figure 9:Signal-background discrimination for theHTH_{T}and AD triggers (CMS Run 283408). (a)HTH_{T}score and(b) AD score distributions for background (MinBias, shaded blue),h→4​bh\to 4bsignal (redsolid), andt​t¯t\bar{t}signal (green dashed). The dashed black line indicates the oracle cut corresponding to theupper edge of the tolerance band (r+=0.275%r^{+}=0.275\%). (c) ROC curves showing signal efficiency versusbackground raterrfor both triggers and both signals. The light blue shaded region denotes the tolerance band(r∗±τ=0.25%±0.025%r^{*}\pm\tau=0.25\%\pm 0.025\%). Markers at the upper band edge show the maximum achievable signal efficiency:HTH_{T}trigger yields 98.0% fort​t¯t\bar{t}and 33.7% forh→4​bh\to 4b, while the AD trigger yields 76.7% fort​t¯t\bar{t}and 41.0% forh→4​bh\to 4b.

Figure 10:(a) GFPO-F for AD trigger (CMS Run 283408) (b) GFPO-FR for AD trigger (CMS Run 283408) (c)h→4​bh\to 4bsignal efficiency for CMS Run 283408 for GFPO-F (inband) and GFPO-FR (inband) and oracle (r+r^{+}) forh→4​bh\to 4b(overall). Theh→4​bh\to 4befficiencyexhibits strong temporal drift, rising from∼11%\sim 11\%in early high-pileup chunks to∼59%\sim 59\%in late low-pileup chunks. Because inbandchunks are notuniformlydistributed but concentrate in the high-efficiency tail of the run as (a) and (b) shows inband chunks tend to concentrate at the tail of the run, the inband average for GFPO-F (42.5%42.5\%) andGFPO-FR (44.5%44.5\%) both exceed the oracle overall average (41.0%41.0\%), which averages uniformly over all chunks excluding first 10 chunks.

Figure 12:DSPOT calibration-deployment mismatch: the GPD is fitted on 50 calibration chunks where the background rate is0.113%0.113\%(AD) and0.150%0.150\%(HTH_{T}) under the fixed initial cut, well below the0.25%0.25\%target, causing a distributional shift of+0.12%+0.12\%and+0.08%+0.08\%at deployment that limits the inband fraction to13.5%13.5\%and17.6%17.6\%over the reamining 24 chunks, while PID tracks the target continuously without burn-in phase.

Figure 13:Zero-feasible step fraction for GRPO and L-GRPO (AD trigger).(a) Mean zero-feasible fraction across all chunks. On MC data, approximately 20% of micro-steps yield no rate-feasible candidate from the sampled groupofGGactions for both GRPO and L-GRPO. On CMS real data this rises to∼\sim61% for both methods. (b) Per-chunk trajectoryover time. The two curves are nearly indistinguishable in both datasets, confirming that the dual variableλt\lambda_{t}modulatesreward weights only and has no effect on the proposal distribution or the fraction of feasible candidates.

Figure 14:Dual variableλt\lambda_{t}and in-band rate over chunks for L-GRPO (AD trigger).(a) On MC data,λt\lambda_{t}decreasesmonotonically from its initial value of 0.25 as the policy stays in-band (∼\sim96% of chunks). (b) On CMS real collision data,λt\lambda_{t}fluctuates but remains roughly constant (≈0.25\approx 0.25) while the in-band rate oscillates around 54%. Shadedregions mark chunks where the in-band rate is below 50%. Despiteλt\lambda_{t}responding to these violation windows, the in-bandrate does not systematically recover, confirming that increasingλt\lambda_{t}amplifies the pathological group update rather thancorrecting the underlying sampling failure.

Figure 15:Background rate trajectory on CMS real data (AD trigger,MC-trained frozen policy).Grey band:±τ\pm\tautolerance around thetargetrB∗r_{B}^{*}. GFPO-F and GFPO-FR track within the band for most ofthe 73-chunk deployment window. GRPO and L-GRPO sit near or below thelower band edge throughout, despite L-GRPO’s dual variable adaptingconcurrently (Figure14). The performance gap isthereforenota matter of insufficient penalty strength but of theGRPO candidate-sampling mechanism failing to produce feasible actionsunder distribution shift.

Figure 16:Zero-feasibility fraction of GRPO vs. group sizeGG.Fraction of training steps on which every rollout violates the rate budget, for AD andHTH_{T}triggers. Even atG=256G=256, 24–34% of steps yield no feasible sample, so the group-relative baseline collapses to an uninformative signal on a constant fraction of updates. ScalingGGalone cannot recover constraint satisfaction, motivating the constraint-aware variants of GRPO in Section4.

Figure 17:GRPO group-feasibility failure (AD trigger, MC,G=16G{=}16); appendix complement to Fig.2.(a) Background rates of allG=16G{=}16candidate rollouts per micro-step;green/reddenotes feasible/infeasible w.r.t. the[90,110][90,110]kHz band. (b) Per-step feasible fractionft=nfeas/Gf_{t}=n_{\text{feas}}/G(run mean⟨ft⟩=0.67\langle f_{t}\rangle=0.67). On 32.08% of stepsft=0f_{t}=0: every candidate lies outside the band, the within-group advantage has zero variance, and the policy receives no feasibility signal. A 67% in-band mean coexisting with a∼32.08%\sim\!32.08\%zero-feasible tail is the regime where group-relative updates fail intermittently, motivating the feasibility-filtered GFPO update.

Figure 18:Step composition for the AD trigger; Complement to Figure4.Categories follow Figure4:Pure feasible(|ℱt|≥K|\mathcal{F}_{t}|\geq K),Padded0<|ℱt|<K0<|\mathcal{F}_{t}|<K),Zero-feasibility(ℱt=∅\mathcal{F}_{t}=\emptyset).GFPO-F/FR achieve57∼73%57\sim 73\%pure-feasible kept sets and pad on14∼21%14\sim 21\%of steps. GRPO is dominated by partial-feasibility (66.95%66.95\%) and zero-feasibility (32.08%32.08\%) steps. The latter matches the value in Figure17and is the failure mode shielded by GFPO.

Figure 19:Look-ahead rates (p1,p2p_{1},p_{2}) and survival ratios (r1,r2r_{1},r_{2})over time, evaluated at each window’s natural operating pointθ=P99.75\theta=P_{99.75}so thatp0≈rB∗p_{0}\approx r_{B}^{*}by construction. Thefour scalars give the agent a discrete forward-rate map of the tail atθ+Δ\theta+\Deltaandθ+2​Δ\theta+2\Delta(withΔ=1\Delta=1GeV onHTH_{T}andΔ=0.5\Delta=0.5on AD), complementing the local sensitivity probe∂r/∂θ\partial r/\partial\theta. Their drift over the run motivates including(p1,p2,r1,r2)(p_{1},p_{2},r_{1},r_{2})as state inputs.

Figure 20:Non-stationarity of trigger thresholds under pileup drift (MC).Top row: The percentile rank of a fixed raw threshold (calibrated to the 99.75th percentile of the first 50k events) drifts substantially over time for bothHTH_{T}(left) and AD (right), meaning the same raw threhsold yields a changing background rate.Bottom row: The normalized thresholdcnormalizedc_{\text{normalized}}where mid and span are derived from the P95-P99.99 calibration range of the first 50k events, illustrates that the ideal operating point (blue) deviates from the fixed calibration value (red dashed line). The idealcnormalizedc_{\text{normalized}}(blue) is calculated as the 99.75th percentile of each 50k events’ score distribution. This is the threshold that would give exactlyr∗=0.25%r^{*}=0.25\%. This suggests includingcnormalizedc_{\text{normalized}}as a state feature so the agent can determine wherectc_{t}sits relative to the score distribution.
Figure 21:Np​vN_{pv}(Number of Primary Vertices) distribution over time (MC).

Figure 22:Exponential Moving Average (EMA) of the fractional rate error at different smoothing factorsλ\lambda, computed over 50k-event sliding windows with a fixed threshold calibrated at the start of the run (MC). The lightblue band indicates the±10\pm 10kHz tolerance around the100100kHz target rate.

header: Appendix GAblation study on noisy anomaly scores

images.caption: Figure 31:UNSW-NB15 TPR by attack difficulty and adaptation regime.For each controller we report TPR on two attack classes:Exploits(frequent, high-signal; easy) andBackdoors(rare, low-signal; hard). Bars span{Deployment,Test-time training}×{Exploits,Backdoors}\{\text{Deployment},\text{Test-time training}\}\times\{\text{Exploits},\text{Backdoors}\}: solid = frozen policy, hatched = online adaptation; high opacity = Exploits, low opacity = Backdoors. GFPO-F and GFPO-FR (ours) maintain the highest Backdoors TPR while satisfying the FAR budget (Fig.5(a)); GRPO and L-GRPO collapse to near-zero TPR via the zero-feasibility failure mode. Test-time or online training widens the GFPO advantage on Backdoors without trading off Exploits.

Figure 32:NAB precision and recall per method (mean over 24 test files).Solid bars: precision; hatched bars: recall. Methods sorted left-to-right by ascending F1. All controllers operate on the same per-chunk anomaly scores under the same FAR budget. GFPO-F and GFPO-FR (ours) dominate the Pareto frontier: precision≈28%\approx 28\%and recall≈42%\approx 42\%. Classical controllers (Constant, PID, DSPOT)reach1111–20%20\%precision but recall below10%10\%, too conservativeto fire on more than a small fraction of true anomalies. PPO, DQN,and the oracle Constant-opt push recall above20%20\%at a precisioncost. GRPO and L-GRPO collapse to the high-precision/near-zero-recallcorner (∼20%\sim\!20\%/2.4%2.4\%): under the sparse anomaly reward, thegroup-relative advantage carries little signal between high-thresholdmodes, and the policy converges to a near-silent threshold.
